components.label: Mobile/Web Client
components.description: Uploads photos via pre-signed S3 URL; reads feed via API

components.label: API Gateway
components.description: Rate limiting, auth, request routing for upload and feed endpoints

components.label: Load Balancer
components.description: Distributes API traffic across app server fleet

components.label: App Server
components.description: Handles upload metadata, feed pagination, like/comment writes

components.label: Fan-out Queue (Kafka)
components.description: Broadcasts new post events to feed-builder workers for follower timeline updates

components.label: Database (PostgreSQL + Cassandra)
components.description: PostgreSQL for user graph and post metadata; Cassandra for feed timelines and activity

components.label: Feed Cache (Redis)
components.description: Stores pre-computed home feed (ordered post-id list) per user; read-through on miss

components.label: Object Storage (S3)
components.description: Stores original photos and all generated thumbnail variants

components.label: CDN
components.description: Delivers photos from edge nodes; supports on-the-fly image resizing

components.label: Monitoring
components.description: Tracks upload throughput, fan-out queue depth, feed cache hit rate, CDN bandwidth

keyInsights: Direct S3 upload with pre-signed URLs offloads all photo bandwidth from app servers
Hybrid fan-out: push to cache for regular users (<10k followers), pull from DB for celebrities (>1M followers)
Feed cache stores only post IDs — full post data fetched in a single batched DB/cache read at display time
CDN image resizing at edge eliminates a dedicated image processing microservice for thumbnails

tradeoffs.decision: Fan-out on write vs pull on read
tradeoffs.pro: Fan-out: O(1) feed reads; feed always fresh in cache
tradeoffs.con: Celebrity posts fan-out to 50M caches — use hybrid pull for mega-accounts

tradeoffs.pro: Scales writes linearly; ideal for time-series feed data
tradeoffs.con: Eventual consistency; no ad-hoc queries; schema must be query-driven

tradeoffs.pro: Eliminates app server as upload proxy; scales infinitely
tradeoffs.con: Client must make two requests (get URL, then upload); slightly more complex client logic

antiPatterns: Routing photo uploads through app servers — saturates server bandwidth at 500M photos/day
Storing photo binary data in the relational database — kills query performance
Generating feeds on every read from the social graph — O(followers) per request at 1B users is catastrophic
Single CDN origin without regional replication — global latency spikes for non-US users
